coredata.dc:description: Recently, sponge biopolymers containing nanoparticles have attracted attention for the preparation of wound-healing products such as wound dressings. Due to the optically non-transparent nature of nanocomposite sponges, only limited microscopic methods have been used to investigate the structural changes caused by adding nanomaterials. The use of various microscopic characterization methods can lead to a better understanding of the performance of nanomaterials in improving the properties of polymer sponges. In this study, photoacoustic microscopy (PAM) imaging was used as a 3D imaging technique to visualize blood penetration and spreading in the fabricated nanocomposite sponges. To form a sponge nanocomposite, carboxymethylcellulose/chitosan (CMC/CS) biopolymers are bonded to green-synthesized carbon dots (CD). The results showed that adding CD to CMC/CS biopolymer and forming CMC/CS/CD nanocomposites caused changes in the cavity structure, resulting in increased porosity and more water absorption and retention, which was better for fluid absorption and therefore more helpful for wound dressing applications. In addition, an investigation of blood penetration and its distribution by PAM showed that by adding CD to composite sponges, blood penetration, diffusion, and absorption increased. Based on these findings, using CD and CMC/CS together can improve the physicochemical properties of sponge nanocomposites, making them ideal for wound dressing.

head.ce:title: Investigating the absorbent properties of carboxymethyl cellulose-chitosan‑carbon dots nanocomposite sponge using photoacoustic microscopy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, sponge biopolymers containing nanoparticles have attracted attention for the preparation of wound-healing products such as wound dressings. Due to the optically non-transparent nature of nanocomposite sponges, only limited microscopic methods have been used to investigate the structural changes caused by adding nanomaterials. The use of various microscopic characterization methods can lead to a better understanding of the performance of nanomaterials in improving the properties of polymer sponges. In this study, photoacoustic microscopy (PAM) imaging was used as a 3D imaging technique to visualize blood penetration and spreading in the fabricated nanocomposite sponges. To form a sponge nanocomposite, carboxymethylcellulose/chitosan (CMC/CS) biopolymers are bonded to green-synthesized carbon dots (CD). The results showed that adding CD to CMC/CS biopolymer and forming CMC/CS/CD nanocomposites caused changes in the cavity structure, resulting in increased porosity and more water absorption and retention, which was better for fluid absorption and therefore more helpful for wound dressing applications. In addition, an investigation of blood penetration and its distribution by PAM showed that by adding CD to composite sponges, blood penetration, diffusion, and absorption increased. Based on these findings, using CD and CMC/CS together can improve the physicochemical properties of sponge nanocomposites, making them ideal for wound dressing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that the skin is the most common and important organ in the human body, and it is essential for protection from invading pathogens [1]. When skin is injured, it loses its protective function and makes the body vulnerable. Wounds need to be protected from environmental irritants until the skin can regenerate, which can be achieved by dressing the wound [2]. Wound dressings constitute an essential component of wound management by forming a physical barrier that prevents the entry of external microorganisms, pollutants, and mechanical injuries into the wound sites [3]. This barrier function significantly reduces the risk of infection and minimizes the possibility of tissue damage or complications. Wound dressings not only regulate moisture levels but also support the healing process. Some wound dressings have special properties that prevent infection or stimulate tissue growth [4]. The choice of wound dressing depends on the type, location, severity, and duration of the wound, as well as the patient's response [5]. Wound dressings can be categorized into various types, such as gauze, transparent, sponge, foam, hydrogel, and hydrocolloid dressings [6]. Some advantages of wound dressings are: that they reduce pain, prevent infection, control exudate, and promote re-epithelialization [7]. Some disadvantages of wound dressings are: that they may adhere to the wound bed, cause peri-wound damage, require frequent changes, and be ineffective for moist wound healing [8]. In light of the flexibility and porosity in wound sponge dressings, which are characterized by the presence of small pores networks in their structure, they emerge as a strong candidate. They can swell significantly due to their open structure, which helps them deal with fluid-leaking wounds. In addition, sponge dressings have a high water uptake capacity that can create a humid microenvironment for wound healing, while inhibiting bacterial penetration into the wound [9,10].

In the last decade, the design and production of nanocomposite sponge wound dressings, which combine biopolymers and nanomaterials, have received increasing attention. Sponge biopolymers containing nanoparticles are a type of nanocomposite material that have potential applications in wound healing [11]. Nanocomposite sponges are porous structures that can absorb and retain fluids, such as blood, and provide a suitable environment for tissue regeneration [12]. Biopolymers for wound dressings have unique features that have intrigued researchers. They are from natural sources such as animals, plants, and microbes, so they outperform synthetic dressings in wound healing [13]. In addition, they have numerous other: being abundant, biodegradable, biocompatible, pain-easing, drug-holding, and chemically modifiable. Dressings composed of Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and Chitosan (CS) have been found to be more effective than traditional dressings in maintaining a moist, non-sticky, and exudate-free wound environment [14]. They also ameliorate the wound healing process and alleviate dress changing. However, the best dressing should be decided by healthcare professionals according to the wound's specific characteristics and needs [15]. Carbon dots (CD) are zero-dimensional carbon-based nanoparticles with diameters of <10 nm that have easy synthesis, remarkable dispersion in water, and favorable biocompatibility [5]. CD can be synthesized from natural sources, such as plants or fruits, using green methods [16]. They can be also used as contrast agents, drug carriers, or photothermal agents for biomedical applications [17,18]. These nanoparticles are superior to other modified carbon dots or other nanoparticles in terms of easy and cost-effective synthesis methods, low toxicity, solubility in water, and hydrophilicity [19,20].

Nowadays, optical imaging methods are widely used in 3D exploration, such as the morphology and structural analysis of biomaterials [16]. Photoacoustic microscopy (PAM) is a versatile technology for biomedical imaging that can provide different types of information about biological samples. It can image the anatomical structures of various organs and tissues at different scales, from a cell to a whole animal [17]. In addition, PAM measures functional parameters such as blood oxygenation, blood flow, and oxygen metabolism without using any labels. It can also detect the presence and distribution of specific molecules or cells by using exogenous contrast agents that have strong optical absorption [18]. PAM is a hybrid optical imaging technique for 3D mapping and evaluating microscopy through the depth of materials even when they are not optically transparent [21]. In PAM, the absorbed light causes the medium to heat up. This heat gives rise to a thermoelastic expansion that promotes an initial pressure. This initial pressure propagates in the medium as an acoustic wave, which is known as a photoacoustic signal and can be detected by an acoustic transducer [22]. This technique can achieve high-resolution images at greater depths than conventional microscopy methods and provide anatomical, functional, and molecular information. PAM can provide optical contrast combined with acoustic detection features, which makes it a versatile tool in biomedical and biomaterial research or preclinical studies [23]. As nanocomposite sponges are not optically transparent, PAM can be a suitable candidate to 3D analysis of the fabricated nanocomposite sponges.

Limited microscopic methods have been used in previous studies, so as to investigate the structural variations of added nanomaterials nanomaterials to the nanocomposite sponges, which have an optically non-transparent nature. The importance of using various microscopic characterization methods is the better understanding of the properties and performance of polymer sponges in the presence of nanomaterials. For this purpose, in this study, PAM was employed as a 3D imaging technique in order to visualize blood penetration and its spreading in the fabricated nanocomposite sponges. This research investigated the synthesis and performance of sponge nanocomposites consisting of CMC and CS natural polymers, and CD nanomaterials for wound dressing. CMC/CS/CDs is a biopolymer-based nanocomposite that combines the properties of CMC, CS, and CDs. By combining these three components, the CMC/CS/CDs nanocomposite sponge can have high absorption capacity, mechanical strength, and hemostatic potential. The morphological and physical properties of the sponge nanocomposite were measured by contact angle measurement, WVTR, water absorption, water retention, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). In addition, mechanical tests were used to evaluate the mechanical properties of the sponge nanocomposite. Sponge samples underwent in vitro testing to assess their blood clotting ability by measuring the blood clotting index (BCI). The blood compatibility and biocompatibility of sponges were investigated under in vitro conditions by measuring their hemolytic activity and cytotoxicity. Finally, in vivo animal experiments were performed to evaluate the hemostatic ability and effectiveness of the samples. In previous studies, due to the optically non-transparent nature of nanocomposite sponges, only limited microscopic methods have been used to investigate the structural changes caused by adding nanomaterial. The use of various microscopic characterization methods can lead to a better understanding of the performance of nanomaterials in improving the properties of polymer sponges. For this purpose, in this study, photoacoustic microscopy imaging was used as a 3D imaging technique to visualize blood penetration and spreading in the fabricated nanocomposite sponges.

The Arjan (Amygdalus reuteri) plant fruit was purchased from an Iranian market. Chitosan (CS, medium molecular weight (MW 193400 Da), 85 % degree of deacetylation), carboxymethyl cellulose sodium salt (CMC, 2500–6000 cps viscosity, average MW 700 kDa, pharmaceutical grade), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Sodium hydroxide, acetic acid, and glycerol (density = 1.255 at 20 °C) provided from Bio Basic Inc. Dulbecco Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), and phosphate buffer saline (PBS) were purchased from the Thermo Fisher.

According to previous reports [24,25], a simple one-step hydrothermal treatment was adopted to prepare CD from the fruit of the Arjan plant. The main reasons for selecting this precursor were abundance, ease of access, and eco-friendliness. 2 g of the precursor was peeled and ground well. It was poured into a stainless-steel autoclave with a Teflon wall along with 20 mL of deionized water and then placed at 200 °C for 4 to 7 h in the oven. A dark brown solution was obtained after cooling to room temperature. To remove bigger particles, it was filtered with filter paper and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 20 min. Finally, the supernatant was collected and stored at 4 °C for subsequent analysis after passing through a 0.22 μm filter.

ATR-FTIR analysis was carried out in transmittance mode with a Tensor 27 (Bruker, Germany) in the wavenumber range of 400 to 4000 cm−1 range using the KBr pellet method to identify the functional groups on the CD surface. The fluorescent image of the CD in the red channel was taken using a Carl Zeiss Jenamed Fluorescence Microscope. In the red channel of this fluorescent microscope, the excitation filter was designed to pass light with a 550 nm–570 nm wavelength. The crystal structure of CD was studied using X-ray diffraction (XRD) with a PANalytical (XPert PRo MPD, Netherlands) at 40 kV, 40 mA, and Cu K radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). The Raman spectra of the CD were recorded at 520 nm as a laser excitation wavelength. The CD solution was applied to a freshly prepared glass plate and allowed to dry for 3 h at room temperature.

The preparation of the nanocomposite sponges followed previously reported protocols [26]. For the preparation, 3 g of CS was initially dissolved in 100 mL of acetic acid solution (1 %) and stirred for 4 h, and a simultaneous 3 g of CMC was dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water and stirred for 4 h. The uniform gel solution was stirred at 500 rpm for 3 h, after the CMC solution was slowly added to the CS solution. Then glycerol (2 % v/v) was slowly added to this homogeneous gel. Then the obtained homogeneous gel was divided into 4 parts. The first part was used to prepare a composite sponge without CD and was named CMC/CS. Then CD solution with different concentrations (1, 2, and 3 % V/V) was added to the other parts of the prepared homogenous gel, and they were well-blended for 1 h and named CMC/CS/CD1, CMC/CS/CD2, and CMC/CS/CD3, respectively. In the following, the above samples were lyophilized overnight by the freeze-drying method. Finally, to obtain CMC/CS composite sponges (CMC/CS/CD1, 2, and 3 nanocomposite sponges), these structures were soaked in a 0.1 M NaOH solution for 1 h to neutralize the pH, then washed with deionized water and lyophilized for another night.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CMC/CS/CD sponge nanocomposites

ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphologies of the samples were examined using an SEM (Tescan, Czech Republic) at 10 kV to observe the homogeneous blending and porous structure of the sponge nanocomposite. The specimens were gold-coated using a sputtering process prior to SEM analysis.

To analyze the existence of CD in the sponge, a confocal fluorescent image of a sponge with CD and without it was taken using a lab-made laser scanning confocal fluorescence microscopy. The confocal images were taken from CD, CMC/CS, and CMC/CS/CD with a 10× objective and the same initial condition. The samples were excited at 561 nm, and their emission at 630 nm was captured.

In order to evaluate and identify intermolecular bonds and functional groups in each sample, FTIR spectroscopy (Tensor 27, Bruker, Germany) was applied. To conduct this study, the sample was combined with pure KBr and formed into a thin tablet of sponge nanocomposites. The IR absorption peaks were then investigated at room temperature by FTIR at wavelengths in the range of 400–4000 cm−1.

A liquid displacement method was used to determine the porosity of the sponge nanocomposites [27]. Sponges were cut into regular cylindroids measuring 2 cm in diameter and 0.5 cm in height. The sponges were then weighed and immersed in absolute ethanol for 1 h at room temperature to allow the ethanol to permeate the sample pores. Following that, the sponges were removed from the absolute ethanol and weighed after being wiped clean of any remaining ethanol on the surface. Each sample was tested three times. Porosity is calculated using the formula:
(1)
P =
m 2 − m 1
ρV
× 100 %
m1 and m2 represent the weight of the sample before and after immersion in ethanol, respectively. V and ρ indicate the sample volume and ethanol density (0.785 g/cm3), respectively.

Weighing 10 mL of deionized water in a cylindrical bottle with a 1 cm2 surface area and a sponge nanocomposite dressing on top of it was used to determine the WVTR. Afterward, it was placed in a 37 °C incubator (Memmert, Germany) with a relative humidity of <20 % for 24 h. Finally, after a certain amount of time had passed the weight of the cylindrical container containing fabricated sponges was measured again, and the WVTR of the sponges was computed using the following formula [28]:
(2)
WVTR =
W 1 − W 2
A
×
10 6
g
m
− 2
d
− 1
W1 represents the system's beginning weight (a cylindrical bottle, water, and sponge wound dressing), W2 represents the system's final weight after reduction, and A represents the bottle cap area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ratio of water absorption to water retention
ce:section.ce:para:
The water absorption capacity of the sponge nanocomposites was determined by the reported method [29]. Before testing, all sponges were freeze-dried and weighed (W0). PBS was used to soak the sponges (pH 7.4). The samples were then removed and carefully dabbed on filter sheets to remove the extra liquid; the weight of the final samples that absorbed the most water was recorded as W1. The water absorption ratio was calculated using the following equation:
(3)
Water absorption
%
=
W 1 − W 0
W 0
× 100
W0 (g) and W1 (g) represent the weight of the sponge before and after PBS absorption (g).

After the samples were fully soaked in deionized water, their water-retention capabilities were assessed based on sample weights at pre-determined intervals. The sponge nanocomposite samples were all the same size and dried for 24 h in a 37 °C oven. For 24 h, the samples were thoroughly swollen in deionized water. The surplus liquid from the surfaces of the swollen samples, whose weights were recorded as the Ws, was removed with filter paper. After that, the swollen samples were heated in an oven at 37 °C for a set amount of time before being weighed again. After that, the Wi was used to record the new weights. Each sample was tested three times in total. The following formula was used to calculate the water-retention ratios of the samples:
(4)
Water retention
%
=
Wi Ws
× 100

The hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity of the sponge was determined by dropping 4 μL of deionized water onto each sample and measuring the contact angle (Ɵ) with a contact angle meter (XCA-50, PMC, Iran). The average contact angle was calculated by calculating the average contact angle of at least three places on each sample [30].

Universal testing equipment (Santam STM-20, Iran) with a 100 kg load cell was used to evaluate the mechanical properties of sponges [2]. Each sample's dimensions were meticulously measured. We examined three sponges, each measuring 4 cm long, 1 cm wide, and 0.2 cm thick, from each sample. The sponges were placed in the jaws of the tensile testing device and then subjected to a constant tensile force at a rate of 2 mm/min until failure.

A lab-made PAM was set up with a lateral and axial resolution of 48 and 27.4 μm respectively. A 532 nm Q-switched laser (MSL-AO-532-500 μJ, CNI Lasers) with an 8 ns pulse width and 1–5 kHz repetition rate was used as a light source. The laser light was split into two directions using a beam splitter, so a small division of laser light was directed towards a fast PD (Silicon PIN Detector, 818-BB-22, Newport) to correct the timing jitter effect and compensate peak power for all signals [31]. The other part of the laser light was coupled to a multi-mode fiber (M29L05 - Ø600 μm, 0.39 NA, Thorlabs) using a coupling lens with a 50 mm focal length. An objective lens with a 50-mm focal length was used to control the laser light focus through the sample and also, direct the light to the acoustic-optic combiner. The acoustic-optic combiner is made up of two right-angle prisms filled with silicon oil, which is transparent for the laser beam and reflective for the photoacoustic signal. An acoustic lens (LC4573, f = −10.0 mm, Ø6 mm, UV Fused Silica, Plano-Concave, Thorlabs) was glued to the bottom of the photoacoustic combiner to focus the photoacoustic signal into a sound detector (V214-BB-RM ultrasound transducer, 50 MHz, Olympus). A water tank with a transparent and flexible membrane in its center was used as the acoustic coupling media. The sponges were fixed in contact with the membrane, and the ultrasonic gel was used to equalize the speed of sound with water (Fig. 1a). An XY stage was used to raster scan the samples on a horizontal plane. The detected photoacoustic signal was converted and processed into a 3D picture using a lab-managed C-Sharp analysis platform (Fig. 1b). In order to have the same initial conditions and examine the repeatability of tests for all samples, a holder with 4 rows and 3 columns was designed. The fabricated holder was CNC milled from a plexiglass sheet. The holder had 12 cylinders, each of which had a 2 mm height and a 4 mm diameter. The sponge samples with a 2 mm height were cut using a 4 mm biopsy punch and fixed in the holder. Then, 5 μL blood was dropped on each sponge sample (Fig. 1c).

The assay was carried out to demonstrate the coagulation efficiency of CMC/CS/CD sponges [32]. In brief, 100 μL of fresh activated blood from rats was dripped on the sponge surface. Blood coagulation on the sponges was activated by adding 20 μL of CaCl2. Then pre-warmed to 37 °C for 10 min. Deionized water (1.5 mL) was used to hemolyze the red blood cells (RBCs), which were not trapped in the clot. Finally, the absorbance of the unclothed blood solution was collected and measured at 540 nm (Abs1). The absorbance of 100 μL of citrated whole blood in 3 mL of deionized water was used as the blank control (Abs0). The blood clotting index was calculated as the following equation:
(5)
BCI
%
=
Abs 1
Abs 0
× 100 %

The hemolysis ratios of CMC/CS/CD sponges were evaluated in vitro. Citrated rat whole blood (1 mL) was centrifuged for 15 min to collect red blood cells (RBCs). Following the addition of 1 mL of rat blood diluted with physiological saline, the tubes containing samples were incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Diluted blood was added to distilled water and 0.9 % saline as positive and negative controls, respectively. The absorbance of the supernatant obtained by centrifuging at 2000 rpm for 30 min was measured by a microplate reader (Bio-Rad Model 550, Hercules, USA) at 545 nm. The hemolysis ratio was calculated using the following formula:
(6)
Hemolysis ratio
%
=
OD
sample − OD
negative
OD
positive − OD
negaative
× 100 %

The cytocompatibility of the sponge dressings was determined using the MTT technique. The MTT assay was used to determine the metabolic activity of cells by converting yellow tetrazolium salt to purple formazan. Before testing, the samples were allowed to swell for 1 h. At 37 °C, each sponge was incubated for 24 h in DMEM with 10 % fetal bovine serum. The extract was then filtered through a 0.22 m microporous membrane and kept at 4 °C until needed. L929 cells were cultured at a density of 10,000 cells per well in 96-well plates. The culture medium was removed after obtaining a monolayer of cells, and an aliquot (100 μL) of the extract was added to the wells. As a control, DMEM with 10 % fetal bovine serum was used. MTT reagent (20 μL) was added to each well after incubation for 24, 48, and 72 h and incubated for 3 h at 37 °C. The liquid in the wells was then drained off, and dimethyl sulfoxide was injected to dissolve the crystals. A microplate reader was used to measure the optical density of each well at 570 nm. Eq. (7) was used to compute the relative cell viability of each sponge:
(7)
Viability
%
=
Sample absorbance Blank absorbance
× 100

The hemostatic ability and effectiveness of the CMC/CS/CD sponges were evaluated by using the rat tail amputation model [33]. Nine mice (with a weight of 180–200 g) were randomly divided into three groups. The mice were anesthetized with 3/7 % chloral hydrate for 3 min before surgery (intraperitoneal injection, 370 mg/1000 g) and then fixed on a surgical tray. All animal experiments were approved by the Research Ethics Committees of Shahid Beheshti University (ethical code number # IR.SBU.REC.1402.012).

The rat tail was then established by amputating the tail with surgical scissors at 4 cm from the tail end. Then the cut surface was placed on a tissue for 15 s to ensure normal blood loss. A CMC/CS and CMC/CS/CD sponge (1 cm3) were lightly pressed against the cut tail. The bleeding time was recorded by a stopwatch. The control group time was the amount of time it took for untreated rat tails to stop bleeding.

The data was presented as a mean ± SD (n = 3). ANOVA test was used to determine significance. P < 0.05 indicated a statistically significant difference. GraphPad Prism statistical software (version 9.0.0) was used for statistical analysis.

CD surface functional groups were identified by FTIR spectra ( Fig. 2a ). The absorption peak at 3337 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibrations of the OH and NH groups [34], and the strong peak at 2922 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of CH. The peak of 1704cm−1 corresponds to the characteristic amplitude CC [35]. On the other hand, the peak at 1461 cm−1 can be identified as the CN, NH, and -COO groups [36]. There is also a strong peak at 1033–1057 cm−1, related to OC stretching vibrations [37]. The FTIR results indicated that the CD has various surface functional groups that might affect their fluorescence and hydrophilicity properties. The surface functional groups include OH, NH, CH, CC, CN, -COO, and OC. These groups could have originated from the precursors or the synthesis process of the CD. The presence of these groups possibly enabled the CD to form hydrogen bonds or covalent bonds with other molecules or substrates [38]. Also, with a fluorescent emission wavelength of 630 nm, CD had a strong fluorescent signal at this wavelength, which proved their intrinsic fluorescence (Fig. 2 b). Fig. 2 c represents the product CD XRD pattern. It has an amorphous structure, with a broad peak at 2Ɵ = 20°, which is clearly related to the amorphous nature of CD [37,39]. Fig. 2 d shows the Raman spectrum of synthesized CD. Raman spectroscopy was studied to confirm the graphitic carbon. The spectrum exhibited two distinct Raman bands at 1372 cm−1 and 1598 cm−1, which belonged to the sp3 defects (D band) and sp2 carbon (G band) in the synthesized CD. The D band was associated with carbon atom vibrations in distorted graphite, while the G band was associated with two-dimensional sp2 carbon atoms. In general, well-graphitized carbon materials have an intensity ratio of D and G band (ID/IG) <1, and the determined ID/IG ratio for the prepared CD was 0.85, indicating that the synthesized CD had fewer defective carbon structures. Basically, the origin of the fluorescence nature of the CD was due to this surface defect. The lower ID values in CD resulted in reduced corrosion and oxygenation processes led to more integrated graphite shells and higher levels of graphitization. Finally, Raman's results confirmed the presence of minor surface defects in the synthesized CD, indicating high quality and mainly composed of graphitic carbon clusters [40,41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CMC/CS/CD sponge nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: CMC/CS/CD sponge nanocomposites were successfully fabricated by using electrostatic interaction and freeze-drying methods. Polyelectrolyte complexes are formed through the interaction of two charged polyelectrolytes, one positive and one negative. CMC, with its opposite electric charge to CS, can react with CS and serve as an ionic cross-linking agent. Due to these interactions, the CMC anionic polyelectrolyte reacted with the CS cationic polyelectrolyte to form the CMC/CS composite. Integrating the CD into a CMC/CS solution potentially led to more complex ionic interactions which assisted in building CMC/CS/CD nanocomposites and promoted the properties of CMC/CS composites. Furthermore, it is likely that the formation of this porous and spongy structure was due to the cross-linking of the hydrogen bond between CD and CMC/CS (Fig. 3a) [42].

The surface and microstructural properties of sponge nanocomposites were observed by SEM. As shown in Fig. 3 b, CMC/CS sponges were white, whereas CMC/CS/CD sponges were uniformly light brown, indicating uniform CD distribution (Fig. 3 c). Porous membranes are suitable for use as wound dressings. The porosity of the dressing enhances the absorption of excess secretions and the cleaning of the wound site [2]. Sponge nanocomposites were found to have an extremely porous structure with voids that were nearly equal in form and size, along with uneven connecting networks. In contrast, the majority of voids in sponge nanocomposites exhibited an interconnected structure. The addition of CD to the CMC/CS sponges resulted in an increase in the pore's number and size, as revealed by comparing the microscopic images of CMC/CS and CMC/CS/CD sponges, as shown in Fig. 3b and c. Porous materials, in general, exhibit low relative density, high specific strength, and a large surface area. They are lightweight, have higher permeability, and can improve composite mechanical properties, including strength and hardness. Additionally, the interconnected pores facilitate cell adhesion, and growth, and provide various mass transfer channels that aid wound healing [1].

To show the incorporation of CD in sponges, a confocal fluorescent image was obtained using a lab-made laser scanning confocal fluorescence microscopy to compare sponges with and without CD. The results revealed a strong fluorescent signal at the 630 nm emission wavelength, while the CMC/CS sponge showed no noticeable fluorescent signal, which represented the extremely weak photoluminescence of carboxymethylcellulose/chitosan at this emission wavelength [43]. On the other hand, the CD and the CD-containing sponge (CMC/CS/CD) exhibited an intense fluorescent signal at 630 nm (Fig. 3d), confirming the presence of nanoparticles in the sponge structure.

FTIR was used to determine the surface functional groups and chemical composition of the produced sponges, as shown in Fig. 3e. The overlapping OH tensile vibration peaks in CMC and N–H/O–H tensile vibration peaks in CS and CD were attributed to a broad peak of around 3281 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of CMC/CS/CD. The O–H/N–H stretching vibration peak in CMC/CS/CD was decreased, most probably due to hydrogen bonds forming in the sponge nanocomposite between CS, CMC, and CD [42]. A small peak was observed at 2881 cm−1, which belonged to the CH2 group. In addition, the peak at 1588 cm−1 for the –NH deformation of –NH2 and the peak at 1411 cm−1 for the amino group were identified. The peak at 1588 cm−1 indicated the presence of amino protonation on the CD surface, potentially impacting its fluorescence properties [44]. The peak at approximately 1031 cm−1 was caused by C–O–C vibrations from CMC. Results indicated the presence of –NH2, –COOH, and –OH groups on the surface of sponges [45]. FTIR results showed that sponges produced from CMC, CS, and CD had different surface functional groups which probably influenced their absorption capacity and degradation behavior.

The porosity of the prepared sponges were measured by using the ethanol transfer method. The addition of CD resulted in a significant increase in the porosity of the CMC/CS sponge from 62 % to 85 % (p < 0.05) (Fig. 4a), which showed the enlargement in size and number of pores and correlated with the SEM image findings. The pore structure and porosity of sponges are widely known to be important elements in wound dressing since they influence the absorption of exudates and blood [27]. Furthermore, the increased porosity facilitates the transfer of nutrients and oxygen to the cells adhering to the dressing [30].

For wound dressing, a hydrophilic surface is essential to make it more biocompatible which can be evaluated by contact angle measurement [46]. A contact angle of below 90° indicates a high affinity of the sponge for water. The chemical composition and topology of the surface determine its wettability. Fig. 4b and c show the contact angles of CMC/CS, CMC/CS/CD1, CMC/CS/CD2 and CMC/CS/CD3 sponges. The contact angle for the CMC/CS sample was decreased from 85.7° to 55.1° for the CMC/CS/CD3 sample. This indicated that the hydrophilic CD enhanced the hydrophilic characteristics of the sponge nanocomposites [35].

An optimal WVTR is critical for a competent wound dress as it prevents both excessive scarring caused by a high WVTR and the accumulation of exudate and bacterial development associated with low WVTR. According to research, the ideal WVTR is 2000–2500 g/m2/day. This range inhibits wound dryness minimizes water loss, and restrains exudate production in the wound bed [47]. As shown in Fig. 5a, the WVTR averaged for samples: blank, CMC/CS, CMC/CS/CD1, CMC/CS/CD2 and CMC/CS/CD3 sponges were (4256 g/m2/day), (1840 g/m2/day), (2073 g/m2/day), (2315 g/m2/day) and (2259 g/m2/day), respectively. The WVTR of sponge nanocomposites was between 2000 and 2500 g/m2 per day; therefore, these sponges were suitable as a wound dress.

These tests are important as they establish a correlation between the physical properties of wound dressings and the biological characteristics of the wound. They help determine the level of wound cleansing and moisturization. In addition, the absorption properties of wound dressings should match the bleeding levels of the wounds. Furthermore, wound moisture retention is widely known to be the optimum setting for successful wound dressing [29]. The water retention capacity of swollen sponges was determined by measuring their weight changes at 37 °C and 50 % relative humidity. CMC/CS sponges exhibited a lower capacity for absorption than CMC/CS/CD sponges. Consequently, adding a certain number of CD to the CMC/CS sponge can improve its water retention rate (Fig. 5b). Fig. 5c and d illustrate the water absorption of sponges. Composite sponges with and without CD had a significant variation in water absorption. With the addition of CD, water absorption increased significantly from 799 % to 868 %, possibly because of the hydrophilicity of the CD and the presence of a group of hydrophilic agents on their surface.

Mechanical characteristics play a crucial role in determining the performance of wound dressings due to their impact on durability, stress resistance, softness, flexibility, and elasticity [46]. Sponge wound dressing tensile strength, elongation at break, and bending strain can be measured to determine their strength and elasticity. As shown in Fig. 6a, the elongation at break of CMC/CS and CMC/CS/CD1, CMC/CS/CD2, and CMC/CS/CD3 sponges was 20.612, 21.447, 22.575, and 22.161 %, respectively. As can be seen, the elongation at break of the CMC/CS polymer chain increased with the addition of CD. This enhancement might have been due to the changes in the crosslink density caused by the CD, which allowed the polymer chains to stretch more. Moreover, according to Fig. 6b, adding CD increased the tensile strength of CMC/CS sponges (p < 0.05) (0.19532 to 0.30979 MPa). The enhanced tensile strength of nanocomposites was attributed to the interaction between CD and CMC/CS polymer chains. The electrostatic interactions that enhanced the tensile strength in nanocomposites seemed to be influenced by the amine and hydroxyl groups in the polymer chains, as well as the surface of the CD. The bending strain was examined in addition to the mechanical qualities of strength and elongation in dressings. The CMC/CS and CMC/CS/CD sponges bending strain capacities are illustrated in Fig. 6c. If the elastic properties of the wound dressing are poor, it may cause blockage in case of excessive pressure and limit the use of the wound dressing [48]. The addition of CD increased the sponges bending strain (10.3648 MPa to 12.3822 MPa, Fig. 6c and d). The increase in mechanical strength in CMC/CS/CD sponges may be the result of the strong electrostatic interaction between the polymer matrix and the CD. Lastly, the mechanical properties of sponges that have been modified by the addition of CD may be utilized in a variety of biomedical applications.

The side views of 3D images captured by PAM are presented in Fig. 7 a–d . The red blood cells have a strong photoacoustic effect, while the sponges do not. The red colour in these side images showed the photoacoustic signals of red blood cells and their absorption from the top to the middle and bottom of the sponge. As can be seen in these 2D images, the depth of the red colour increased with enhancement in the concentration of CD in sponges, indicating improvement of blood absorption upon CD addition. In Fig. 7 e, the blood penetration in side-view images was measured and its average was plotted for each type of sample. The velocity of sound in the blood (1578.2 m/s) was assumed as the sound velocity. As can be seen in Fig. 7 f and g, the height of blood penetration in CMC/CS/CD was significantly increased compared to CMC/CS. Most likely, the increase in absorption and penetration of blood into CMC/CS/CD sponges was due to the presence of hydrophilic CD groups on the nanocomposite surfaces. In addition, another possible reason for these results is related to the increase in the porosity of the sponges with the addition of CD, as mentioned earlier. These results were consistent with the water absorption results.

As mentioned before, a red blood cell has great photoacoustic signals while a sponge does not. Therefore, the increase in the photoacoustic signal intensity represents the aggregation of blood on the sponge. In order to examine the blood aggregation, the maximum amplitude projection (MAP) of 3D photoacoustic images from the top is presented in Figs. 9a-d. As it was evident from the images colour scale, the red colour indicated the higher intensities of the photoacoustic signals, while the blue colour represented the lower intensities. Qualitatively, the CMC/CS samples exhibited higher intensities, indicating more blood aggregation on these sponges. In order to quantitatively characterize the blood accumulation in sponges, the pixels were counted by setting 85 as the threshold intensity, and then the frequency percentage above 84 in each MAP images was calculated; the average value of this percentage is displayed in Fig. 9e [
51
,
52
]. This percentage decreased significantly with the increase in CD concentration, so the addition of CD to sponges enhanced their ability to distribute blood more uniformly. Also, the addition of CD reduced the accumulation of red blood cells on the CMC/CS/CD sponges. As schematically can be seen in Fig. 9f, for the same volume of blood injection, the sponges with CD had more uniform and lower intensities, which meant the blood aggregation was lower and the blood spread and penetrated better in the CMC/CS/CD. This was consistent with the SEM data presented earlier, which showed that the sponge nanocomposites with CD had less spreading on the surface and more penetration than those without CD. such results were attributed to the varying degree of crosslinking in the CMC/CS/CD sponge, which led to more and larger pores in their structure.

The in vitro coagulation capacity of the sponges was characterized by whole blood clotting and quantified by the blood clotting index (BCI). This index directly reflected the clotting ability of hemostatic dressings and the measurement of hemoglobin absorbance at 540 nm. A lower BCI value indicates better clotting efficiency [32]. As a result, in Fig. 10a and b, the indexes for CMC/CS, CMC/CS/CD1, CMC/CS/CD2, and CMC/CS/CD3 sponges were 23.06, 20.69, 18.74, and 15.14, respectively. According to the results, increasing the concentration of CD increased the hemostatic potential of the sponge. The three-dimensional porous structure of sponges along with the mutual adhesion of chitosan and blood cells, promoted the clumping of red blood cells and activated the coagulation system. The inclusion of CD might encourage platelet enrichment and hasten the coagulation process [53]. The FWHM of the samples showed that the width of the peaks increased with the CD concentration in the sponges. This increase in FWHM indicated an improvement in blood absorption ability, which ultimately increases the hemostatic potential of CMC/CS/CD sponges.

The measurement of hemolysis is based on the amount of erythrolysis and hemoglobin separation when the sponges come into contact with blood. In order to further explore the biocompatibility of CMC/CS/CD sponges, we evaluated their hemolysis. Generally, the lower hemolysis rate indicates a higher blood compatibility. Based on ASTM F756–17: Standard Practise for Assessment of Hemolytic Properties of Materials, the samples with an HR of <5 % are considered to have high blood compatibility [54]. According to the results, all sponges showed a low hemolysis ratio, below 5 %, which implied favorable compatibility with blood. This was because the high porosity and specific surface area of composite sponges facilitated effective contact with blood, which accelerated the blood coagulation and reduced the hemostasis time (Figs. 10c) [55]. Also, according to PAM images, it was possibly due to better diffusion and penetration of blood in the pores of CMC/CS/CD sponges.

Biocompatibility is considered an essential requirement for medical and biomedical applications of sponge nanocomposites. The biocompatibility of the sponge nanocomposites with skin cells, as indicated by cell viability in their presence, suggested their suitability for wound dressing applications [56]. The cytotoxicity of sponges against L929 cells was determined in vitro using the MTT assay (Fig. 10 d). The percentage of cell viability in CMC/CS/CD sponges slightly decreased after 24 h of incubation compared to CMC/CS and control. Also, CMC/CS/CD had a minor effect on cell viability compared to CMC/CS. Finally, in the 72 h incubation period, the percentage of cell viability decreases to nearly 85 % in the CMC/CS/CD3 sample. The CMC/CS exhibited higher cell viability rates than CMC/CS/CD during the 72 h period. The results showed that CMC/CS/CD sponges slightly reduced the growth of L929 fibroblast cells, but cell viability varied between 85 % and 97 % for all incubations. Basically, samples with cell viability >75 % are considered non-cytotoxic and are desirable materials for wound dressings [57]. The MTT test results showed that CMC/CS/CD nanocomposite sponges were well-compatible and exhibited no toxicity with fibroblast cells. These findings suggested their potential as promising candidates for wound dressing applications.

Hemostasis, the initial phase of cutaneous wound healing, begins following a skin injury. This primary hemostatic process initiates a secondary hemostatic coagulation cascade involving fibrinogen conversion to insoluble fibrin. Eventually, fibrin threads aggregate with platelets to form a blood clot [58,59]. In the earliest stages of an injury, the hemostatic agent is a crucial component of the surgical arsenal and must have a greater capacity for blood absorption. Utilizing the rat tail amputation model, the hemostatic capacity and efficacy of CMC/CS/CD sponges were evaluated [33]. The hemostatic time of the rat tail model was 464 s for the control group, 188 s for the CMC/CS sponges, and 138 s for the CMC/CS/CD sponges (Fig. 10 e and f). The control group suffered the greatest blood loss among the studied groups. According to the in vivo hemostasis performance studies, CMC/CS/CD sponges demonstrated excellent hemostasis properties. During the hemostasis process, CMC/CS/CD sponges exhibited a high ratio of volumetric expansion, as well as blood absorption, and a rapid shape recovery performance. After being placed on the wound, the sponge rapidly expanded and applied pressure to stop the bleeding. CMC/CS/CD sponges could also rapidly collect red blood cells and platelets to accelerate the clotting process, resulting in effective hemostatic properties. Subsequently, the results of PAM images also showed that increasing the concentration of CD decreased the accumulation of red blood cells on the surface and increased blood penetration and the blood spreading into the sponge. These results, in turn, showed that CMC/CS/CD sponges were able to collect and absorb red blood cells and platelets, thereby accelerating the coagulation process.

In the presence of CD, the homeostatic ability of nanocomposite sponges increased. Probably, the cationic part of chitosan and CD can form a viscous blood-like gel and increase blood absorption [26]. In fact, positively charged chitosan has inherent antimicrobial and hemostatic properties by stimulating vascular contraction, and it can also stop bleeding by interacting with negatively charged platelets and red blood cells to form a blood clot [58].

This work aimed to develop biodegradable sponges for wound dressing using natural materials. The sponges consisted of chitosan (CS), a biocompatible biopolymer, and carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), a polymer that increased the strength of the sponges. Green carbon dots (CD), derived from natural sources, were also incorporated into the sponges to enhance their porosity and hydrophilicity. The sponges exhibited a good balance of absorbency and mechanical properties due to the synergistic effect of these components. Various techniques were employed to characterize the structure, morphology, composition, and functionality of the sponges. It was demonstrated that the addition of CD to CMC/CS biopolymer improved the porosity, water absorption and retention, and blood penetration of the sponges. Photoacoustic microscopy (PAM) was used to visualize the blood diffusion and absorption within the sponges, which confirmed the positive effect of CD. These findings indicate that the sponges have potential applications in wound healing. However, further studies are needed to assess the biocompatibility and biodegradability of the sponges in vivo. Moreover, the optimization of the CD synthesis and the sponge fabrication processes, as well as the evaluation of the sponges in different wound scenarios, are suggested as future research directions. One of the limitations was the use of a single type of green carbon dots, which may have limited the diversity and functionality of the sponges. Other types of carbon dots, such as those derived from different natural sources or modified with different functional groups, may have different or better effects on the sponges. Thus, future studies should explore the use of various carbon dots to optimize the properties and applications of the sponges.

Neda Aminoroaya: Investigation, Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Hamide Ehtesabi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Reza Monfared-Hajishirkiaee: Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.
